segments.text: Welcome back. I hope you were able to get a coffee during that break. I'm Jeremy Khan,
Fortune's AI editor again, and I'm going to be moderating this next session. This is kind
of in three parts. So the last one was on sort of the what of AI. This is on the why and the why
now, kind of the business case for AI agents. But I'm just going to give a quick overview of where we
are. I'm going to try to give some different data than I did in the last little intro. This is
some data from OpenAI's evaluation called GDP Val. And if you haven't looked at it, it's really
worth looking at because they had professional experts in a whole range of knowledge work,
develop realistic kind of scenarios and tests for to try AI models, frontier AI models against.
And it ranges from sort of things in banking, things in tax, things in audit, government services,
and some in engineering tasks as well. And this is some of the data from industrial engineering and
mechanical engineering from the last assessment they did. And this showed and they do a whole bunch of
they don't just test their own models, they test other frontier models as well. Currently it looks
like most of the best models are just over sort of 50% wins and ties against human performance.
Again, this is judged by human experts. And when it comes to mechanical engineering, the models
actually tie human performance or exceed human performance 60% of the time. So they're coming along
really quite quite significantly. And it's worth keeping an eye on that benchmark. Of course,
one of the other things we've seen in the last few months has been the real rapid adoption and
thusiasm for AI agents, in particularly these what are called agentic harnesses, this kind of software
code that sits around an AI model and tells it kind of what tools it's allowed to use and what
scenarios provide some guard railing. How many here are familiar with OpenClaw or have played around
with OpenClaw? Quite a few, that's good. Yeah, there's been huge enthusiasm for OpenClaw as a
agentic harness. And a lot of people now are trying, but the problem with OpenClaw is actually
it was fairly unsafe. You kind of had to give it control over everything on a laptop and there were
lots of nightmare scenarios where OpenClaw had gone out and done things that the users hadn't really
wanted, including in some cases giving up bank account information or the keys to Bitcoin wallets,
things like that that you don't really want to have happened. So a bunch of people now are working
on how do you bring an OpenClaw like experience to enterprises in a way that's safe for enterprises.
This is still very much kind of a work in progress, but it is something to keep an eye on.
In terms of what's actually happening in companies, there's a lot you may have heard, there was this
MIT study that came out earlier this year saying that a lot of companies were failing to achieve
return on investment from their use of AI, there was a statistic that 95% of AI pilots fail.
I think we're kind of moving beyond that. I think that study is already fairly outdated.
And you are seeing some really big enterprises seeing really significant productivity gains
through the use of these agentic AI processes. This is a case study from Novo Nordus, the big pharmaceutical
company that worked with Anthropic with their cloud model. They were able to take a process that
used to take more than 10 weeks that had to do with compiling all of this data from clinical trials
for these clinical trial reports that they then have to present actually to regulators.
They took that down from 10 weeks to 10 minutes. So that's a huge savings that we're seeing.
We're also starting to see the rise of these sort of solo entrepreneurs who are using AI to create
huge companies with massive revenue with almost no employees. So this is a guy who runs a kind of
medical supplement marketing company The New York Times wrote up. He's got $1.8 billion
of users and annual revenue. He has two employees. So I mean these are the sorts of things that people
are starting to see is possible with AI agents. And I think there's a big, a lot of big companies
are saying how do we bring a little bit of that inside our own enterprise? What can we possibly do
inside this much larger company that already may have billions of revenue to extend to new business
opportunities? We're going to talk a little bit about this opportunity with my next set of
panelists. Please welcome Andreas Mauer, the chief architect from Bosch of building solutions.
Thomas Titsa, I hope you haven't mascured all your last names. The technical technology strategist
at Microsoft and Nevin who is the VP of platform strategy and technology partnership at Autodesk.
Please welcome. I'm not even going to try Nevin's last name. What is...
Yuxsel Ikiji. Yuxsel Ikiji. So I should have gotten that's great. Thank you. Welcome. Please.
Great. Andreas, I want to start with you because you have this fantastic vision for what you think
is going to be possible at Bosch building solutions with the advent of AI and AI agents. Can you just
sketch for us a little bit of what that vision looks like? Because I think it's tremendously exciting.
Yeah, thanks. So at Bosch building technologies we creating commercial building solutions systems
and services like you see here with light and HVAC in those commercial buildings and the fan
fact is that these buildings are not really standardized in terms of how you operate them
and even how do you plan dimension and design them so that you can bring them into those buildings
and this is where we are now leveraging more and more the power of a generic system and trying
to get that system design and planning and dimensioning so that we can create offers to customers
using the power of a generic systems. That's one of the pieces. Then once we have that system
designed and planned and offered to customers then we also bring them into the building. So we do
provisioning installation configuring and this is also where we use the generic systems behind the
scenes to get assisted configurations going, assisted installation for the engineers and the
craftments that install all of this in the buildings. Then next we bring it into operation and then
we operate, we maintain it and then sometimes Bosch building getting refurnished, restructured,
sometimes even recycled and that is when the loop closes and starts from the beginning.
All of those processes life cycle faces you need to have a high domain expertise and those people
getting less and less. So we have a shortage of them and all of those affairs are not really
highly standardized and this is also where our partnership with all the desk comes in and also
the partnership with Microsoft. We're trying to civilize the wild west of commercial buildings as
I always say and we have a big milestone today as well. We have a joint white paper composed,
you find it in the flyer, we call this the open building service reference. This is trying to
articulate, you can download the flyer as a QR code, this is really trying to articulate our dilemma
that we have in those commercial buildings because some of you may know 30% of the CO2 footprint
are caused by those buildings and the reason is they are not standardized, proactively operated,
designed, constructed and this is where we use the whole range of everything we heard so far
and there's even the new phrase called agent tile which is the combination of a tentic plus HIL.
So because Ben was just deluding on what is the next kind of HIL look like, it's called agent tile
and we are in the midst of this. Wow, that's a, no it's an amazing vision. One of the things even on
that first piece on the system design you were talking about is that right now if you come to
Bosch and you want a request for proposal for a fire alarm system for a building, it takes some
time for Bosch to come up with that, it was a people intensive process and that limited the number
of those that you could do in a given period of time and that itself limited the market opportunities
that you could go after. You could only do a building of a certain size with a certain valuation.
Now by automating that you can not only sort of take on more projects but also potentially serve
market segments that you just couldn't serve before. Is that that's part of the vision? Maybe
you can talk a little about what how that works. Yeah, absolutely. So we in this white paper you will
also find something that we call the system plan our co-pilot. I give you some numbers. So in Germany
alone we're getting per week 100 tender requests from customers for fire alarm systems and to
plan such fire alarm systems depends of the complexity depends up on the side size. So it's
a difference if you have a soccer stadium or an airport or a railway station or a building like
this and so usually we cannot serve all of those 100 tender requests. So we lose business.
And so by automating that now with a tentic system so we're getting all of those tender requests
specification documents in including usually 2D plans. Never or seldomly do you get 3D
build models. It's always 2D something. It's PDF, it's chain pack, it's maybe DVG, all of those
funny formats. Sometimes it's even scanned in paper maps. And what we're now doing we really
are transforming that in a whole pipeline of what people used to do in the process. So we take the
information models we receive. We do the scoring. Is that business relevant for us? Yes, no. If
that light goes green, we do the bill of material based up on product catalogs that comes from
integrated enterprise master data management systems. Is a set of agents that are educated for
that given task attend. And the next thing is what we're doing now stunningly is we're transforming
to 2D maps we receive into 3D using the auto desk agents on the other side. So we're having an agent
to agent integration. We create a 3D building model and then we design and project a fire alarm
system in real time into that 3D model. And that is not just stunning and cool. It is also
changing our industry. Because we're getting away from that wild west, what somebody thought
should be planned and how it's being installed later is now in this fully life cycle chain,
highly standardized from agents assisted and guaranteed to be safe. And that of course gives us
higher business throughput. It gives us our standardizations and therefore we also make our
business far more efficient. And we just start with a fire alarm domain. We have many other
domains like HPEC, like interruption detection, like access control, video surveillance, energy
management obviously which is a very important one. And so this is one of the examples,
quite exciting times in industry. Yeah, never. I want to bring you in. When we were preparing for
this panel you mentioned that a lot of companies when they first start thinking about using AI or
using AI agents, they kind of look at their existing processes and they just try to make them a
little bit faster, a little bit more productive. And you said that the danger is that you kind of end
up with a lot of faster horses when you really want is a rocket ship. Can you talk a little bit about
about that and how you avoid maybe that trap of falling into that sort of faster horses mentality?
Yeah, and I think that's exactly right. I think when you start with, okay, these are the processes
and the people and the outputs we have right now. And how do we apply AI to this? Like in our
software development process, for example, can we use AI to automate documentation? We can,
but that's getting you a faster horse. If you rethink the, okay, what outcome are we actually
trying to get to? And how do we rethink that with these capabilities? Ben talked about that in
the previous panel in terms of totally rethinking the process. Now what used to take the longest
time was the coding, right, and software development. And that is, you know, you can now automate that
with agents, but that changes the whole dynamic because now you could automate some
pretty terrible mic outcomes, not really get to the outcome you want if you don't have the
specifications very, very detailed. So now actually the bottleneck shifts, for example, to
describing exactly the outcome you want. And then you can skip a few steps with agents. And then
now testing also becomes very important, right? So the whole mindset needs to change from the top
down. And it can't just be like a few, you know, excited engineers, like they on their own,
they will not make such a big shift at the whole company. So rethinking it really requires a
mindset shift at even leadership levels. Absolutely. Thomas, I want to bring you in here. What,
you know, when you see customers maybe get stuck at that POC stage, you know, what is usually
the issue there? Why do some of these projects fail to scale? And when you have seen ones that
really have been successful and or really ambitious, like what Bosch is trying to do, what, what
do they need to have done to sort of lay the foundation for that? And we see this very often,
to be honest, that you start a lot of POCs, people build their own chat pilots and so on. And
what then is missing really the transformation to the business? So what does it mean for my business
process? I have to probably rethink my business process. What people will do in the future in this
process. And then I have to think about, okay, what is the platform to host this? Do I have a
scalable platform? If I use this, not in just one process, maybe in 100 process, if the
chat pilot is not used by 10 people, by 10,000 people, or in the case of Bosch, 100,000 people maybe,
can I scale? Do I have the security and so on? And we have to rethink also traditional IT
processes. Can we adapt this processes for this new world? And can we bring this capabilities
then also to all employees, not just the engineers who are used to use this? What happens if we bring
to administrators to sales guys and so on? And how can we train them to use these tools? But we
often see you provide tools for like a salesman and they are not used to use it. This training
missing and so on. So you also have to invest in trainings and explain these things to your users.
Andreas, how are you dealing with that at Bosch in terms of making sure people know how to use
these tools effectively? Yeah, I mean, it always sounds like when I hear those C people talking
about the 10X, 100X kind of savings, there is a real hard practice behind that you have to
understand. To get as Thomas said in early POC, that is fairly fast done. But to bring this into
a materialized highly automated engineering practice, you need to establish TeneiOps. TeneiOps
is the same as this as DevOps does, but for the Tenei practice. And that ensures that you have
end-to-end observability, which is very important. So you want to know who agent talks to other
agents, what models are used, how many tokens did they consume, which prompts the day use,
and what was the response in the collaborative interaction between agents. So you need to have
this entire observability from the human operating with the digital agents all the way to the results
and outcomes. And at Bosch, of course, we need to have this all and I guess for enterprise
corporations as well, we need to have this compliant. So you need to adhere to ethical AI,
responsible AI, transparent AI, ethical AI, right? Then you have funds like the FunFacts in Europe,
like the EU AI Act that you have to adhere to. So to bring this all in practice, really, really,
you have to take this seriously, an easy POC, that's, yeah, it's wipe, as they say. But to bring
this materialized into an industry practice takes really effort and skills. So yes, you have now
digital beings that do standardized and help you to get higher business process efficiency.
But in order to get there, you first have to invest. And there's also a fun fact behind that,
without data strategies and data architectures, you can forget it. You better go and have a nice
dinner with your wife instead. Because if you don't have data strategies and data architecture,
then the AI is pretty much powerless, I would say. So you have to respect all of this and
you have to invest for this and tell this to your C people because it takes, it costs money.
Right. Yeah, and I want to pile on there with the building that trusts its infrastructure at the
beginning. We've really invested a lot there to avoid kind of a wild west approach because every
product team could have like gone and developed something. But it creates a lot of tech debt.
And also how do you ensure the compliance, security, reliability that we commit to for our
customers because we're operating industries where that matters. And so we don't want to skimp on
that. So what we did was invest a lot in that trusted foundation first, really providing that
visibility to our customers in terms of AI transparency cards for each feature,
building a common shared infrastructures to make sure that each of our AI capabilities are built
in a secure, governed, compliant way. And that actually really, that upfront investment
really helped us speed up later because now each of the, like any, any team that wants to build an
AI feature as long as they use that shared infrastructure, they can just go and they don't have to
worry about all that stuff. It comes built in as long as you built it in that central place. And
that really helps us speed up then. So it feels like you go slow, but you go slow to go fast.
Go slow to go fast. That's interesting. Yeah, and there's one other hidden secret test
ability because if you have non-ditaministic systems, there's no such thing like a unit test.
So you need to establish domain specific evaluation pipelines. And these evaluation pipelines actually

And so you better have also in your data strategy semantics included, meaning you have ontologies

absolutely. And that is something you have to include in your data strategy. And thankfully,

how to use the evaluation pipelines to stock evaluation pipelines like you want to check if the

At Bosch, how did you decide where to start? I mean, you have this vision of, you know, so much

Unfortunately, there's not one answer which works for everyone in the company. Your salesman needs

and where they should start with thinking about these agentic processes, what would it be?
Yeah, I mean, identify your most critical business areas that you want to standardize and optimize,
and also think about the level of autonomy you want to give those agentic systems. Because that is

so you can hire a super agent and you have to learn what spectrum development means because we
no longer trying to have abstractions to talk to compute machines. We now express intent.
And there are machines that will turn the intent around to talk to abstract the things like
